properties.name: Pushkar Camel Fair

facts: World's largest camel fair — 25,000+ camels traded annually across a 12-day mela ending on Kartik Purnima.
Held on the banks of Pushkar Lake — India's only continuously-worshipped Brahma-associated freshwater tirth.
The lake is fringed by 52 ghats and 500+ temples — Rajasthan's densest sacred architecture.
Kartik Purnima ritual bath on the lake is believed to grant moksha equal to yagnas conducted over centuries.

properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajasthan geography

properties.name: Beneshwar Fair

facts: Rajasthan's biggest tribal fair — often called the 'Kumbh of the Bhils' — held at the Mahi-Som-Jakham triveni sangam.
The Beneshwar Mahadev shrine here is the only Shiva temple in India where a naturally-broken (khandit) shivling is worshipped.
Draws over 200,000 tribal pilgrims from Rajasthan, Gujarat and Madhya Pradesh over the 5-day Magh Purnima mela.
Rituals combine Bhil animist tradition, Vaishnavite Nishkalank Sant Mavji reformism, and Shaivite Beneshwar worship.
Held on Magha Purnima at the confluence (sangam) of the Mahi, Som and Jakham rivers in Dungarpur.
Associated with a Swayambhu (self-manifest) Shivlinga at the Beneshwar Mahadev shrine.
Called the "Kumbh of the tribals" — the largest tribal fair in India, drawing Bhil pilgrims from Rajasthan, Gujarat and Madhya Pradesh.

properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajasthan geography

properties.name: Kapil Muni Fair, Kolayat

facts: Held at Kolayat Lake — the traditional site where Sage Kapila (Kapil Muni) meditated and founded the Sankhya school of Indian philosophy.
Kartik Purnima ritual bath and lamp-floating on the sacred lake — one of Rajasthan's oldest continuously-held fairs.
The Kolayat Ashram houses over 40 shrines and dharamshalas around the lake perimeter.
Draws pilgrims from the Rajasthan-Sindh-Punjab-Haryana Yogi and Nath traditions.
Held on Kartik Purnima at Kolayat (Bikaner district) — a five-day fair centred on the Kolayat lake.
Associated with Sage Kapil Muni, founder of the Samkhya school of Hindu philosophy.
The holy dip in Kolayat lake is believed to remove sins — the site is linked to Puranic legends of the sage's tapasya.

properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajasthan geography

properties.name: Karni Mata Fair, Deshnok

facts: Twin fairs held at the Karni Mata Temple, Deshnok — the tutelary Kul Devi (family goddess) of the Bikaner Rathores.
The temple houses ~25,000 protected black rats ('kabas'); spotting the rare white rat is considered auspicious.
Karni Mata is worshipped as an incarnation of Durga; the deity is credited with foretelling the Bikaner kingdom's rise.
Rao Bika founded Bikaner in 1488 after receiving Karni Mata's blessing at this site.
Held at the Karni Mata temple in Deshnok during Chaitra and Ashwin Navratras.
Famous as the "Temple of Rats" — the sacred kabas are believed to be reincarnated Charan family members.
Karni Mata is the Kuldevi (family goddess) of the Rathore rulers of Bikaner and Jodhpur.

properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajasthan geography

properties.name: Baba Ramdev Fair, Ramdevra

facts: Rajasthan's biggest folk-deity pilgrimage — draws over 10 million pilgrims each Bhadrapada.
Ramdev Pir (1352-1385) was a Tomar Rajput saint worshipped by all castes and both Hindus and Muslims (as 'Ramshah Pir').
The Ramdevra samadhi is believed to grant relief from paralysis and skin ailments; pilgrims walk barefoot from all directions.
Ramdev's five 'panch pir' companions — including the Meghwal Dalit saint Harji Bhati — are worshipped alongside him.
Held annually at Ramdevra (also called Runicha) near Pokharan — the samadhi of Baba Ramdevji, a Rajasthani folk deity revered across caste lines.
A major pilgrimage fair drawing lakhs of devotees from Rajasthan and neighbouring states, walking barefoot from as far as Gujarat.

properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajasthan geography

properties.name: Kaila Devi Fair

facts: Rajasthan's fourth-biggest fair — draws over 4 million pilgrims each Chaitra Navratri.
Kaila Devi is the tutelary goddess of the Karauli Yadav ruling clan (traced descent from Krishna).
The fair spans 15 days; the temple sits at the base of the Trikoot hill, on the Kalisil river.
Lakhs of devotees perform the 3-km barefoot 'lakh danva' (100,000 steps) around the temple perimeter.
Held on Chaitra Shukla Ashtami at the Trikuta hills in Karauli.
Called "Lakkhi Mela" because pilgrim numbers cross a lakh.
The temple houses two idols — Kaila Devi (Mahalakshmi form) and Chamunda — worshipped side by side.

properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajasthan geography

properties.name: Mallinath Cattle Fair, Tilwara

facts: Rajasthan's oldest continuous livestock fair — held annually on the banks of the Luni at Tilwara.
Commemorates Rawal Mallinath (14th c.), a Rathore prince-saint of Marwar and the founder-saint of the Malani region.
Nagauri, Tharparkar, Kankrej and Rathi cattle — Rajasthan's traditional draught breeds — traded here.
The 15-day mela hosts folk performances (langa-manganiar), camel races and folk-cricket alongside cattle trade.

properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajasthan geography

properties.name: Nagaur Cattle Fair

facts: Asia's second-largest livestock fair after Pushkar — 70,000+ cattle, camels and horses traded across 8 days.
Anchored on the Nagauri breed of bullock — Rajasthan's premier draught cattle, prized for pulling power.
Held on the Bhilwara-Nagaur road at Ramdev Pashu Mela grounds; state government stalls promote AI insemination.
Sub-fairs: Mirchi Bazaar (chilli mandi) and Wooden-Article Bazaar are among Asia's largest.

properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajasthan geography

properties.name: Shitala Mata Fair, Chaksu

facts: Held at Shitala Mata temple, Chaksu (35 km south of Jaipur) — Rajasthan's biggest fair for the smallpox-goddess.
The fair is a week-long women's ritual event — bassi-bhojan (cold food offerings) mark the goddess's cooling nature.
Shitala Ashtami (7 days after Holi) is the traditional starting day.
Historic: the temple predates Jaipur city (1727) by centuries; Chaksu was a Kachhawaha stronghold before Amber.
Held on Chaitra Krishna Saptami-Ashtami at Sheet Dungri near Chaksu (Jaipur).
The temple was built by Maharaja Madhav Singh of Jaipur.
Known as the "bullock cart fair" — the mela includes a large animal fair alongside the pilgrimage.
Sheetla Mata is worshipped as the protectress against diseases like smallpox, chickenpox and measles.

properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajasthan geography

properties.name: Guru Jambeshwar Fair, Mukam

facts: Twin fairs at Mukam (Bikaner) — the samadhi shrine of Guru Jambheshwar (1451-1536), founder of the Bishnoi community.
Jambhoji laid down 29 (Bish-noi) principles of environmental ethics — the world's earliest documented conservation ethic.
Mukam draws all Bishnoi lineages from Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana, MP and Delhi.
The Khejarli martyrs (1730 khejri-tree massacre) are commemorated alongside Jambhoji at Mukam.

properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajasthan geography

properties.name: Shri Mahavir Ji Fair

facts: Rajasthan's biggest Jain fair — draws over 500,000 Jain pilgrims each Chaitra Purnima.
The Shri Mahavirji temple houses an idol of the 24th Tirthankara believed to have been miraculously discovered at this spot.
The 8-day mela combines Digambara Jain rituals with tribal Meena participation — a rare Jain-tribal syncretic event.
The 'ratha yatra' (chariot procession) on the Gambhiri river is the mela's climax.
Jain fair held from Chaitra Shukla Trayodashi to Vaishakh Krishna Pratipada at Chandanpur (Karauli district).
The largest Jain fair in India — draws pilgrims from every Jain sect.
Features a chariot procession (rath yatra) and a ritual where descendants of a tanner community lead the first pull — a tradition traced to the idol's discovery legend.

properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajasthan geography

properties.name: Chandrabhaga Fair, Jhalarapatan

facts: Rajasthan's biggest cattle-and-buffalo fair after Nagaur — held on the Chandrabhaga river at Jhalarapatan.
Kartik Purnima ritual bath in the Chandrabhaga is the mela's opening ceremony.
The 10th-c. Padmanabh Sun Temple and Shitleshwar Mahadev Temple frame the fair grounds.
Malwa-plateau cattle breeds (Malvi, Nimadi) dominate the trade — Rajasthan's largest fair-market for these varieties.

properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + standard Rajasthan geography

properties.name: Jeenmata Fair

facts: Held at the Jeenmata temple in Rewasa village (Sikar) during Chaitra and Ashwin Navratras.
The goddess is depicted with eight arms; lamps of ghee and oil are said to have burned continuously in her sanctum for centuries.
Linked to the folk tale of the sibling deities Harsha and Jeen — devotees visit for the fulfilment of wishes.

properties.source: Rajasthan Current Affairs Today (June 2026), pp. 7-8

properties.name: Khatu Shyamji Fair

facts: Dedicated to Shyamji, a form of Krishna, revered here as "Sheesh ke Dani" (the giver of his head).
The principal mela runs from Phalgun Shukla Dashami to Dwadashi and attracts devotees year-round.
The Shyam Kund water tank and the temple garden are the two main draws beyond the sanctum.

properties.source: Rajasthan Current Affairs Today (June 2026), pp. 7-8

properties.name: Bhartari Fair

facts: Held twice a year in Vaishakh and Bhadrapada in Alwar district.
Commemorates King Bhartrihari (Bhartari) who renounced his throne to become an ascetic.
A "mini Kumbh" — sadhus and jogis from across north India gather with traditional appearance and disciplines.

properties.source: Rajasthan Current Affairs Today (June 2026), pp. 7-8

properties.name: Diggi Kalyanji Fair

facts: Held on Shravan Amavasya at Diggi village in Tonk district.
Dedicated to Lord Vishnu, worshipped here in the form of Kalyanji.
Linked to the legend of King Digva who was cured of leprosy by the divine idol.

properties.source: Rajasthan Current Affairs Today (June 2026), pp. 7-8

properties.name: Tejaji Fair, Parbatsar

facts: Held at Parbatsar (Nagaur) in honour of the folk deity Tejaji — a Jat hero venerated across rural Rajasthan.
Central to Tejaji devotion — devotees seek protection from snake bites, invoking his final promise to return in serpent form.
Includes one of Rajasthan's largest cattle fairs, held alongside the religious gathering.

properties.source: Rajasthan Current Affairs Today (June 2026), pp. 7-8

properties.name: Pabuji Fair, Kolumund

facts: Held at Kolumund / Kolu Pabuji village in Phalodi district — the birthplace of the folk deity Pabuji.
Associated with the heroic traditions of the Pabuji Ki Phad — a scroll-narrated epic of cattle-raiding and honour.
Bhopa priests from the Nayak community perform the phad rendition through the night as part of the mela ritual.

properties.source: Rajasthan Current Affairs Today (June 2026), pp. 7-8

properties.name: Gogaji Fair, Gogamedi

facts: Held at Gogamedi (Hanumangarh) in honour of Gogaji, the folk snake-deity — devotees also visit his birthplace Dadrewa (Churu).
Reflects the rural faith in protection from snake bites and cattle disease — Gogaji is often invoked alongside Tejaji.
Cross-community following — the shrine has both a Hindu temple and a Muslim tomb (mazar) at the site.

properties.source: Rajasthan Current Affairs Today (June 2026), pp. 7-8

properties.name: Sitabari Fair

facts: The principal tribal fair of the Sahariya community, held at Sitabari in Shahabad tehsil (Baran).
Sitabari is associated with Sita's exile — Valmiki's ashram and Lav-Kush's birthplace are placed here in local tradition.
A major religious and cultural gathering of Sahariya society — the tribe has PVTG status in Rajasthan.

properties.source: Rajasthan Current Affairs Today (June 2026), pp. 7-8
